title: Whether city councils should commission public art without a public vote
summary: Argue whether the decision to commission public art should belong to expert panels and elected officials, or require a public vote before any work is installed.
topic: Whether city councils should commission public art without a public vote
prompt: Write an argumentative essay (350–500 words) examining whether city councils are justified in commissioning public art — sculptures, murals, installations — without holding a public vote, or whether placing permanent works in shared spaces requires direct democratic consent. Consider questions of aesthetic expertise, civic ownership, and the danger of design-by-committee. Use a cleft sentence, a concession ('Granted, …' or 'Admittedly, …'), at least one passive construction, and a participle clause. Include at least one concrete example. Take a clear position.
hintStarters: What a public vote on public art actually measures is not aesthetic quality but…
Admittedly, the history of publicly commissioned art is not without its embarrassments — works that…
Rejected by local residents in initial surveys, the Angel of the North was eventually…

hintVocab.meaningVi: hợp đồng nghệ thuật công cộng
quyền sở hữu công dân / cảm giác thuộc về cộng đồng
chuyên môn thẩm mỹ
thiết kế theo kiểu ủy ban (thường dẫn đến kết quả tầm thường)
hội đồng chuyên gia
kích thích cuộc tranh luận công khai
một tác phẩm nghệ thuật lắp đặt vĩnh cửu
trao quyền hạn cho
mẫu số chung thấp nhất (tiêu chuẩn thấp nhất mà mọi người chấp nhận)
bệ tượng
hành động phá bỏ / phủ nhận các biểu tượng truyền thống
tạo ra sự đồng thuận
một tác phẩm mang tính biểu tượng / nổi bật
bản sắc đô thị
làm thuần hóa / vô trùng hóa thị hiếu công chúng
phán đoán của người phụ trách triển lãm / giám tuyển
bị tranh cãi bởi cư dân
điêu khắc hoành tráng / kích thước lớn
giá trị nghệ thuật
tính hợp pháp dân chủ

sampleText:
Public space is, by definition, owned by everyone. The argument that city councils should therefore consult the public before placing permanent art within it has an intuitive appeal: if the sculpture stands outside my library for the next forty years, do I not have a right to weigh in? The appeal, though, dissolves under examination.

What a public vote on aesthetic questions tends to produce is not collective wisdom but collective caution. The Angel of the North — Antony Gormley's rusted steel sculpture in Gateshead — was met with significant local hostility before installation. Residents who had been consulted in preliminary surveys were largely opposed: it was too big, too industrial, too strange. Installed in 1998 nonetheless, it is now regarded as one of England's most recognised landmarks, visited by over one hundred and fifty thousand people annually and cited as having transformed the region's self-image. Had the final decision been put to a binding vote, it is difficult to believe the Angel would have been commissioned at all.

The deeper issue is structural. Public votes on aesthetic choices systematically favour the familiar over the challenging — not because the public lacks taste but because taste is cultivated through exposure. A population that has not yet seen a work of public art cannot evaluate it; they can only project their existing preferences onto it. Asked to vote on a proposed sculpture, most people will instinctively prefer the recognisable bronze figure over the abstract steel installation, because the former resembles things they have already been taught to accept. The vote, in this sense, measures not what residents want but what they have already encountered.

Admittedly, vesting authority entirely in expert panels produces its own failures. Works have been commissioned — at considerable public expense — that are experienced by the communities they inhabit as alien, irrelevant, or even contemptuous. Having been designed in a studio far from the neighbourhood where it stands, a sculpture can feel like an imposition rather than a gift. The accountability of the commissioning process matters.

Granted, public consultation is not the same as a binding vote. Architects, curators, and artists who engage meaningfully with communities before a commission is finalised — not to determine the outcome but to inform it — tend to produce works that generate deeper local attachment. The problem is not that residents should have no voice; it is that aesthetic decisions made by majority rule tend to sanitise public taste rather than expand it. The city deserves better than its first instinct.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: What-cleft to highlight a structural problem with majority voting
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What a public vote on aesthetic questions tends to produce is not collective wisdom but collective caution' dùng cấu trúc 'What … is …' để đặt sự đối lập trực tiếp ở cuối câu. Cụm 'not collective wisdom but collective caution' được đặt ở vị trí nhấn mạnh nhất, biến lập luận thành một tuyên bố sắc bén thay vì một nhận xét trung lập.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What a public vote on aesthetic questions tends to produce is not collective wisdom but collective caution' uses the what-cleft to place the sharp opposition at the end — the most emphatic position. The contrast 'not collective wisdom but collective caution' lands at the end of the sentence, turning the argument into a precise claim rather than a neutral observation.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Mixed conditional to signal a counterfactual consequence
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Had the final decision been put to a binding vote, it is difficult to believe the Angel would have been commissioned at all' dùng cấu trúc điều kiện pha trộn: 'Had + past participle' (điều kiện loại 3 trong mệnh đề if) kết hợp với 'would have been' (hệ quả loại 3). Điều này hình dung một quá khứ khác với thực tế — một công cụ mạnh mẽ trong văn phong nghị luận để chỉ ra điều gì có thể đã bị mất.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Had the final decision been put to a binding vote, it is difficult to believe the Angel would have been commissioned at all' uses a mixed conditional: 'Had + past participle' (third conditional if-clause) combined with 'would have been' (third conditional result). This imagines a past different from reality — a powerful argumentative move to show what might have been lost.

sampleTranslationVi:
Không gian công cộng, theo định nghĩa, thuộc sở hữu của mọi người. Lập luận rằng các hội đồng thành phố do đó nên tham khảo ý kiến công chúng trước khi đặt nghệ thuật vĩnh cửu trong đó có sức hấp dẫn trực giác: nếu bức tượng đứng trước thư viện của tôi trong bốn mươi năm tới, tôi không có quyền lên tiếng sao? Tuy nhiên, sức hấp dẫn đó tan biến khi xem xét kỹ.

Điều mà một cuộc bỏ phiếu công khai về các câu hỏi thẩm mỹ thường tạo ra không phải là trí tuệ tập thể mà là sự thận trọng tập thể. Bức tượng Angel of the North — tác phẩm điêu khắc thép gỉ của Antony Gormley ở Gateshead — đã vấp phải sự phản đối đáng kể của địa phương trước khi lắp đặt. Cư dân đã được hỏi ý kiến trong các cuộc khảo sát sơ bộ hầu hết phản đối: nó quá lớn, quá công nghiệp, quá kỳ lạ. Được lắp đặt vào năm 1998 bất kể, hiện nay nó được coi là một trong những địa danh nổi tiếng nhất của nước Anh, được hơn một trăm năm mươi nghìn người đến thăm hàng năm và được dẫn chứng là đã biến đổi hình ảnh tự nhận của vùng. Nếu quyết định cuối cùng đã được đưa ra bỏ phiếu ràng buộc, khó tin rằng bức tượng Angel sẽ được đặt hàng ngay từ đầu.

Vấn đề sâu hơn mang tính cấu trúc. Các cuộc bỏ phiếu công khai về các lựa chọn thẩm mỹ một cách hệ thống ưu tiên những gì quen thuộc hơn những gì thách thức — không phải vì công chúng thiếu thị hiếu mà vì thị hiếu được hình thành thông qua sự tiếp xúc. Một cộng đồng chưa từng thấy một tác phẩm nghệ thuật công cộng không thể đánh giá nó; họ chỉ có thể chiếu sở thích hiện có của mình lên nó. Được yêu cầu bỏ phiếu về một bức điêu khắc được đề xuất, hầu hết mọi người theo bản năng sẽ thích nhân vật đồng màu nâu có thể nhận ra hơn là tác phẩm lắp đặt thép trừu tượng, vì cái trước giống những thứ họ đã được dạy chấp nhận. Cuộc bỏ phiếu, theo nghĩa này, không đo lường những gì cư dân muốn mà là những gì họ đã từng gặp.

Thừa nhận rằng, trao toàn bộ quyền hạn cho các hội đồng chuyên gia cũng tạo ra những thất bại riêng của nó. Các tác phẩm đã được đặt hàng — với chi phí công cộng đáng kể — mà các cộng đồng nơi họ sinh sống trải nghiệm như xa lạ, không liên quan hoặc thậm chí coi thường. Đã được thiết kế trong một xưởng vẽ xa khu dân cư nơi nó đứng, một bức điêu khắc có thể cảm thấy như một sự áp đặt hơn là một món quà. Trách nhiệm giải trình của quy trình đặt hàng quan trọng.

Thừa nhận rằng, tham vấn công khai không giống như một cuộc bỏ phiếu ràng buộc. Các kiến trúc sư, giám tuyển và nghệ sĩ tương tác có ý nghĩa với các cộng đồng trước khi hợp đồng được hoàn thiện — không phải để xác định kết quả mà để thông báo cho nó — có xu hướng tạo ra các tác phẩm tạo ra sự gắn kết địa phương sâu sắc hơn. Vấn đề không phải là cư dân không nên có tiếng nói; mà là các quyết định thẩm mỹ được đưa ra theo quy tắc đa số có xu hướng làm thuần hóa thị hiếu công chúng hơn là mở rộng nó. Thành phố xứng đáng được nhận nhiều hơn bản năng đầu tiên của nó.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a what-cleft to highlight a structural consequence of public votes?
options: A public vote on aesthetic questions produces what is not collective wisdom but collective caution.
Collective caution rather than collective wisdom is what a public vote on aesthetics produces.
What a public vote on aesthetic questions tends to produce is not collective wisdom but collective caution.
Not collective wisdom, what a public vote tends to produce, but collective caution.
mcQuestions.explanation: The what-cleft 'What [subject + verb] is [contrast]' places the key contrast — wisdom vs. caution — in the most emphatic final position.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What … is …' with the contrast as the final element.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a mixed conditional to imagine a different past?
options: If the final decision was put to a binding vote, the Angel would not have been commissioned.
Had the final decision been put to a binding vote, it would be difficult to believe the Angel will be commissioned.
If the final decision had put to a binding vote, it is difficult to believe the Angel would have been commissioned.
Had the final decision been put to a binding vote, it is difficult to believe the Angel would have been commissioned at all.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Had + past participle' in the condition and 'would have been' in the result is the standard third conditional (counterfactual past) structure.
mcQuestions.hint: A counterfactual past needs 'Had + past participle' in the if-clause — not 'was' or 'had put'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which passive construction correctly foregrounds what happened to local residents?
options: Residents who had been consulted in preliminary surveys were largely opposed to the sculpture.
The city council consulted local residents in preliminary surveys before the Angel was installed.
Preliminary surveys, consulted by residents, showed that most were opposed to the sculpture.
Residents consulted in preliminary surveys largely opposing the sculpture were told to reconsider.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Who had been consulted in preliminary surveys' uses a passive relative clause to foreground the residents' experience — that they were the recipients of the consultation process.
mcQuestions.hint: Which sentence makes the residents the grammatical subject of a passive verb?

options: A professional design process managed by an elected city council
A process in which a group of people with conflicting preferences produces a bland or mediocre outcome through compromise
The method by which public artworks are approved by a panel of qualified critics
A technique in which designers take turns presenting their ideas to a client
mcQuestions.explanation: Design by committee is a pejorative phrase for a creative process dominated by group decision-making — the compromises required to satisfy everyone tend to produce safe, uninspiring results.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of what happens when everyone on a committee has veto power — what kind of design survives?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a fronted participle clause to establish a prior condition?
options: A sculpture that was designed in a studio far from the neighbourhood can feel like an imposition.
A sculpture can feel like an imposition rather than a gift, having been designed far away.
Having been designed in a studio far from the neighbourhood where it stands, a sculpture can feel like an imposition rather than a gift.
Designed far from its neighbourhood, having been in a studio, the sculpture feels like an imposition.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Having been designed in a studio far from the neighbourhood where it stands' is a fronted perfect passive participle clause — it establishes a prior condition before the main clause consequence.
mcQuestions.hint: The fronted participle clause must come before the main subject and establish something that happened first.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'civic ownership' mean in the context of public art?
options: Legal title to a public artwork held by the city council on behalf of residents
The civic duty to pay taxes that fund public art commissions
A certificate of ownership issued to artists whose work is installed in public spaces
A sense of personal investment and belonging that citizens feel toward shared public spaces and what is placed in them
mcQuestions.explanation: Civic ownership is a sense of emotional and communal investment — citizens feeling that the public space is theirs and that they have a stake in how it looks and what it contains.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'ownership' not as a legal document but as a feeling — what does it mean to feel the street is 'yours'?

options: Admittedly, vesting authority entirely in expert panels produces its own failures.
Vesting authority in expert panels, admittedly, produces its own admittedly failures.
Expert panels, admittedly, authority vesting in them entirely produces failures its own.
Admittedly expert panels authority vesting is producing its own failures entirely.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Admittedly,' is followed by a complete clause in normal subject-verb order — the standard form.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'Admittedly,', look for a grammatically complete clause in standard word order.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'the lowest common denominator' mean?
options: The simplest mathematical fraction that can represent two quantities equally
The most basic or least ambitious standard that satisfies the broadest possible group
The least popular option in a democratic vote
The minimum legal requirement that a public artwork must meet
mcQuestions.explanation: In social contexts, the lowest common denominator refers to the most unambitious option that offends or excludes no one — often the result of designing for the widest possible audience.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of it mathematically first — then apply the logic to group taste: what standard is low enough that everyone can accept it?

options: A financial bonus paid to artists who win a competition
The popularity of a work of art as measured by visitor numbers
The degree to which a work of art demonstrates genuine creative, aesthetic, or intellectual quality
The legal right of an artist to receive payment for their work
mcQuestions.explanation: Artistic merit refers to the inherent quality of a work — its creative ambition, technical skill, and cultural or intellectual significance — as distinct from its popularity or commercial success.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Merit' means deserved quality — what kind of quality is being assessed when we evaluate art?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best pivots from a concession back to the main argument?
options: For instance, several poorly received public artworks have been removed following community pressure.
Similarly, expert panels in other fields also make decisions without public referenda.
In addition, the cost of commissioning public art is itself a source of public controversy.
Granted, public consultation is not the same as a binding vote.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted,' acknowledges the force of the counter-argument and then pivots to distinguish between meaningful consultation and a binding majority vote — a key refinement of the essay's position.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option opens a distinction between two things that might be confused — consultation and a binding vote?

options: The authority that a decision or institution derives from genuine consent or participation of the people it affects
The legal right of an elected government to raise taxes
The requirement that all public artworks receive approval from the national government
The principle that only democratically elected politicians may make aesthetic decisions
mcQuestions.explanation: Democratic legitimacy is the authority that comes from genuine public consent — a decision has democratic legitimacy if it was made through a process that the affected people recognise as fair and representative.
mcQuestions.hint: What makes a decision 'legitimate' in a democracy — who grants that legitimacy?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence maintains the most formal academic register?
options: People voting on art is basically a disaster because they'll just pick the safest and most boring option every time.
The majority-rule model of aesthetic decision-making tends to produce conservative choices that reflect existing preferences rather than expand them.
Obviously, you can't let regular people decide what art goes up in public because they don't know enough about it.
Voting on art is kind of controversial because some people think experts should decide and others think it should be up to everyone.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'The majority-rule model of aesthetic decision-making', 'conservative choices', and 'reflect existing preferences rather than expand them' are precise, formal collocations appropriate to academic argument.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid 'basically', 'obviously', 'kind of', 'regular people', and 'up to everyone' — these are informal.

options: A legal decision made by a court about the ownership of an artwork
The opinion of a museum visitor about the quality of an exhibition
The professional expertise and discretion of a curator in selecting, interpreting, and presenting artworks
The financial assessment of an artwork's market value
mcQuestions.explanation: Curatorial judgement refers to the informed, professional decision-making of a curator — someone with expertise in art, context, and audiences — in selecting and presenting works.
mcQuestions.hint: A curator is a professional who makes informed choices about art — what kind of judgement does that professional expertise produce?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses inversion after a restrictive negative adverb?
options: Rarely a majority vote produces the kind of artistically ambitious outcome that transforms a city's identity.
A majority vote rarely does produce the kind of ambitious outcome that transforms a city's identity.
Rarely transforming a city's identity, does a majority vote produce artistic ambition.
Rarely does a majority vote produce the kind of artistically ambitious outcome that transforms a city's identity.
mcQuestions.explanation: After 'Rarely', the auxiliary ('does') must precede the subject ('a majority vote') — subject-auxiliary inversion after a negative or restrictive adverb.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'Rarely', does the auxiliary come before or after the subject?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to sanitise public taste' mean?
options: To produce only safe, inoffensive, conventional aesthetic choices that do not challenge the public's existing preferences
To clean and restore artworks damaged by public exposure to the elements
To educate the public about the importance of hygiene in public spaces
To remove offensive or controversial artworks from public display
mcQuestions.explanation: To sanitise taste means to make it risk-free and uncontroversial — eliminating the challenging, unfamiliar, or provocative in favour of what everyone already accepts.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of sanitising as removing anything 'dangerous' — what is 'dangerous' in aesthetic terms, and why might that be a loss?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase best paraphrases 'the city deserves better than its first instinct'?
options: Cities should invest more money in public art than they currently do.
The initial, unconsidered response of a community should not be the final word on what art it receives.
City councils deserve more trust from their residents when making aesthetic decisions.
A city's first attempt to commission public art is rarely successful and should be repeated.
mcQuestions.explanation: The phrase argues that the first, instinctive reaction to an unfamiliar artwork — which tends to be cautious or resistant — should not determine what a community ultimately receives.
mcQuestions.hint: 'First instinct' is the unexamined gut reaction — what does saying a city 'deserves better' suggest about that reaction?

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a piece of public art in a city you know — a sculpture, mural, or monument. When you first saw it, what was your reaction? Has that reaction changed over time or with more exposure? Does your experience support or challenge the essay's argument that taste is cultivated through exposure?
